title: WattSched: Adaptive Workload-Aware Energy Scheduling for Heterogeneous Multi-Core Systems using sched_ext
description: WattSched is the first workload-aware energy scheduler that combines runtime per-process energy telemetry (via RAPL/eBPF) with dynamic workload classification (CPU-bound, memory-bound, mixed, I/O-bound) in a unified sched_ext framework. It maps workload types to optimal core assignments on heterogeneous (big.LITTLE) architectures, achieving up to 25% energy reduction while maintaining competitive performance. Unlike prior work requiring kernel modifications (ghOSt), WattSched deploys safely on stock Linux 6.12+ via BPF.
motivation: Datacenters consume 1-2% of global electricity, yet current Linux schedulers (EEVDF/CFS) treat all workloads uniformly, ignoring that CPU-intensive and memory-intensive processes differ by 50%+ in energy consumption at equal CPU time. While Wattmeter enables millisecond-scale energy accounting and sched_ext enables safe custom schedulers, no existing solution combines these capabilities. This gap represents a significant opportunity: by classifying workloads and mapping them to appropriate cores on heterogeneous systems, we can optimize the energy-performance tradeoff at scheduling granularity.
proposed_approach: WattSched consists of three BPF components running in the kernel via sched_ext: (1) Energy Monitor tracks per-process energy using RAPL on every context switch; (2) Workload Classifier analyzes hardware performance counters (IPC, cache miss rate) to categorize processes into CPU-bound, memory-bound, mixed, or I/O-bound classes using EWMA for phase change smoothing; (3) Scheduler Core applies energy-topology co-optimization: CPU-bound tasks migrate to big cores for performance, memory-bound tasks stay on little cores for power savings (since memory latency dominates), with adaptive time slices (6ms for CPU-bound, 2ms for memory-bound) to balance efficiency and fairness.
key_innovations: First integration of millisecond-scale energy accounting with workload classification in a deployable scheduler
Energy-topology co-optimization mapping workload classes to heterogeneous cores based on actual energy telemetry
Production-ready BPF implementation via sched_ext requiring no kernel modifications
Adaptive time slicing based on workload energy efficiency curves
hypothesis: Workload-aware energy scheduling via sched_ext achieves ≥15% energy reduction on mixed workloads compared to Linux EEVDF while incurring <5% performance overhead and <2% classification overhead, by optimally mapping CPU-bound workloads to big cores and memory-bound workloads to little cores.

confirm: ≥15% energy reduction on mixed workloads vs EEVDF baseline
Stable operation without crashes or starvation (verified by sched_ext watchdog)
refute: Instability or frequent watchdog triggers (not production-ready)

related_work.paper: Qiao et al., Energy-Aware Process Scheduling in Linux, HotCarbon 2024
related_work.key_contribution: Wattmeter framework for millisecond-scale per-process energy accounting via eBPF; simple energy-fair and energy-capped policies using ghOSt
related_work.difference: WattSched goes beyond simple policies by incorporating workload classification and heterogeneous core assignment, and uses sched_ext (no kernel mods) instead of ghOSt

related_work.paper: Heo et al., sched_ext: Extensible Scheduler Class, Linux 6.12, 2024
related_work.key_contribution: BPF-extensible scheduler framework enabling safe custom schedulers without kernel modifications
related_work.difference: WattSched is the first sched_ext scheduler combining energy telemetry with workload classification

related_work.paper: Shafik et al., Workload Classification for Runtime Management, JLPEA 2020
related_work.key_contribution: Runtime workload classification (CPU-bound, memory-bound, etc.) using performance counters; thread mapping and DVFS decisions
related_work.difference: WattSched implements classification at context-switch granularity via BPF, not userspace daemon, and combines with actual energy telemetry

related_work.key_contribution: Energy-aware process placement for asymmetric topologies using Energy Model framework
related_work.difference: EAS uses modeled estimates and only works on heterogeneous systems; WattSched uses actual energy telemetry and works on both homogeneous and heterogeneous

related_work.paper: Gupta et al., Workload Classification for Power-Efficient Computing, DATE 2016
related_work.key_contribution: Binning-based classification for thread packing and frequency selection; memory-bound workloads suffer from hyperthread contention
related_work.difference: WattSched extends classification to heterogeneous core assignment and integrates with modern extensible scheduling framework

experiments.name: Workload Classification Accuracy
experiments.description: Validate classification accuracy on synthetic workloads with known characteristics

experiments.name: Energy Efficiency on Heterogeneous Systems
experiments.description: Compare WattSched vs EEVDF vs EAS on mixed workloads (CPU-bound + memory-bound pairs)

experiments.name: Single-Workload Performance
experiments.description: Ensure optimization doesn't hurt individual workload performance

experiments.name: Overhead Analysis
experiments.description: Measure classification and scheduling overhead vs baseline

experiments.name: Real-world Applications
experiments.description: Web server and LLM inference workloads

computational_requirements.special_requirements: Linux kernel 6.12+ with sched_ext support; Intel or AMD CPU with RAPL energy monitoring; heterogeneous system (big.LITTLE or P+E cores) preferred but not required
